model.description: MiMo • The flagship foundation for the agentic era, built for complex, long-horizon, multi-step agentic tasks.

pick.rationale: Arkansas holds overwhelming analytical advantages with a KenPom rank of 17 and Torvik rank of 20, featuring elite offensive efficiency (rank 5) that should exploit Hawai'i's weaker defensive profile. The Razorbacks' experience in the SEC, recent tournament momentum from winning the conference championship, and significant talent disparity make them heavy favorites. While Hawai'i earned a 13-seed by winning the Big West, their lack of comparable metrics and the 15.5-point spread indicate a substantial gap, making a 4/13 upset unlikely.

reasoningStep.title: Arkansas vs Hawai'i Matchup Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Arkansas is the clear favorite based on superior ratings, conference strength, and betting market indicators.
evidence: Arkansas KenPom rank 17, Torvik rank 20, with elite offense (rank 5) and solid defense (rank 50).
Hawai'i lacks KenPom/Torvik ratings, suggesting a weaker analytical profile as a Big West champion.
ESPN Analytics gives Arkansas 91.6% win probability; betting line is Arkansas -15.5.
Arkansas is healthy and confident after winning the SEC tournament; no major injury concerns reported.
Historical 4 vs 13 seed matchup favors the higher seed approximately 80% of the time.

result.text: Arkansas basketball notebook: Trevon Brazile looks to be X-factor for Razorbacks in March Madness | The Arkansas Democrat-Gazette - Arkansas' Best News Source --> --> --> --> Subscribe Sign In Donate Manage Subscription Sign Out Donate Today's Paper Election 2026 Local News Puzzles Archives Newsletters Legal Notices Opinion Funded Stories Obits Arkansas basketball notebook: Trevon Brazile looks to be X-factor for Razorbacks in March Madness 5 hours, 29 minutes ago by Ethan Westerman --> --> --> --> Arkansas forward Trevon Brazile maneuvers in the paint, Wednesday, March 18, 2026, during practice for the first and second rounds of the NCAA Men's Basketball Tournament at Moda Center in Portland, Ore. (Hank Layton/WholeHogSports) Let us read it for you. Listen now. Your browser does not support the audio element. PORTLAND, Ore. — When Trevon Brazile buried a late 3-pointer that punctuated a victory over Vanderbilt in the SEC Tournament Championship Game on Sunday , John Calipari giddily jogged down the sideline. For the Arkansas men’s basketball coach, the celebration was deeper than a trophy coming back to Fayetteville. He saw a glimpse of how far the Razorbacks can go in March. In a 54-second stretch, Brazile did everything — knocked down another 3, sprinted back on defense and rose up to deny an alley-oop — flashing the version of himself the Razorbacks will need if they’re going to make a deep NCAA Tournament run. During the sequence, Brazile cracked a smile coming back to the huddle as Calipari greeted him with a hug. “He's been great,” Brazile said. “He pushes me every day. Even when things are going good, he always still has something to … help me [improve] myself. I just thank him for that.” The Razorbacks (26-8) are relying on the 6-foot-10 big man to continue being an X-factor for them in the NCAA Tournament. Without his playmaking, the Big Dance could be a short one. “TB wouldn't shoot the ball,” Calipari said in a video posted earlier this week to Hogs+ . “We're all in the huddle saying, ‘What? Shoot the ball. We're all good. Whatever happens, I'm going to take responsibility anyway.’” During an 85-81 win over LSU in late January, Calipari told Brazile he wanted him to throw the dagger with a late 3-pointer . He did the same thing in the Vanderbilt game, dialing up the game-sealing shot during a timeout. “If the play is run for me to shoot it, I’ve got to shoot it,” Brazile said. “[Calipari] saying that obviously is going to bring some confidence. He's 2 for 2 right now, as far as calling it.” Arkansas forward Trevon Brazile (7) and head coach John Calipari (right) react after a made 3-pointer defended by Vanderbilt guard Tyler Tanner, Sunday, March 15, 2026, during the second half of the Razorbacks' 86-75 win over the Commodores in the SEC Tournament championship at Bridgestone Arena in Nashville, Tenn. (Hank Layton/WholeHogSports) With a matchup against No. 13 seed Hawaii (24-8) known for its height and length looming, Brazile has an o

result.text: Arkansas basketball notebook: Trevon Brazile looks to be X-factor for Razorbacks in March Madness | The Arkansas Democrat-Gazette - Arkansas' Best News Source --> --> --> --> Subscribe Sign In Donate Manage Subscription Sign Out Donate Today's Paper Election 2026 Local News Puzzles Archives Newsletters Legal Notices Opinion Funded Stories Obits Arkansas basketball notebook: Trevon Brazile looks to be X-factor for Razorbacks in March Madness 5 hours, 29 minutes ago by Ethan Westerman --> --> --> --> Arkansas forward Trevon Brazile maneuvers in the paint, Wednesday, March 18, 2026, during practice for the first and second rounds of the NCAA Men's Basketball Tournament at Moda Center in Portland, Ore. (Hank Layton/WholeHogSports) Let us read it for you. Listen now. Your browser does not support the audio element. PORTLAND, Ore. — When Trevon Brazile buried a late 3-pointer that punctuated a victory over Vanderbilt in the SEC Tournament Championship Game on Sunday , John Calipari giddily jogged down the sideline. For the Arkansas men’s basketball coach, the celebration was deeper than a trophy coming back to Fayetteville. He saw a glimpse of how far the Razorbacks can go in March. In a 54-second stretch, Brazile did everything — knocked down another 3, sprinted back on defense and rose up to deny an alley-oop — flashing the version of himself the Razorbacks will need if they’re going to make a deep NCAA Tournament run. During the sequence, Brazile cracked a smile coming back to the huddle as Calipari greeted him with a hug. “He's been great,” Brazile said. “He pushes me every day. Even when things are going good, he always still has something to … help me [improve] myself. I just thank him for that.” The Razorbacks (26-8) are relying on the 6-foot-10 big man to continue being an X-factor for them in the NCAA Tournament. Without his playmaking, the Big Dance could be a short one. “TB wouldn't shoot the ball,” Calipari said in a video posted earlier this week to Hogs+ . “We're all in the huddle saying, ‘What? Shoot the ball. We're all good. Whatever happens, I'm going to take responsibility anyway.’” During an 85-81 win over LSU in late January, Calipari told Brazile he wanted him to throw the dagger with a late 3-pointer . He did the same thing in the Vanderbilt game, dialing up the game-sealing shot during a timeout. “If the play is run for me to shoot it, I’ve got to shoot it,” Brazile said. “[Calipari] saying that obviously is going to bring some confidence. He's 2 for 2 right now, as far as calling it.” Arkansas forward Trevon Brazile (7) and head coach John Calipari (right) react after a made 3-pointer defended by Vanderbilt guard Tyler Tanner, Sunday, March 15, 2026, during the second half of the Razorbacks' 86-75 win over the Commodores in the SEC Tournament championship at Bridgestone Arena in Nashville, Tenn. (Hank Layton/WholeHogSports) With a matchup against No. 13 seed Hawaii (24-8) known for its height and length looming, Brazile has an o
